note: David's first slide-rating pass. Stopped labelling the repetitive story-* slides partway (many nulls in the source export). Rated subset preserved here; full export incl. unrated lives in browser localStorage key mocha-slides-ratings-v1.

ratings.label: I like the step details, how the 4 has dots and the 6 has yellow background, it breaks things up
I like the use of color for each group, I like the clean pills on the right and the command file names color and mono text
love the ying/yang, when you have two concepts this is cool
Bad contrast on the dark brown, I like the key command as a green block, and I don't mind the use of James when skip James.
I find this slide deck shape interesting, but I don't want it all the time. It's almost something I don't want to ask for, but this is a nice version of a slide deck.
I like my key commands. I also like how the branding colours, especially the yellows, are used. The little pill that appears for the headlines (or commands) is really cool.

This is interesting mainly because of the randomness. The fact that something came through pink like this isn't on brand and doesn't make sense from a semantic colour perspective. Just speaking my mind; don't know how much I'd want that in the real world.
I really love that this is trying to show different stages, and they all get their own colour section. It's not overpowering.

I like the AppyDave colours. That is my original design idea: the light brown and yellow on dark brown. It looks really good. Also like 0 to AT with seven BMAD agents in white, and 'What you'll learn' in brown underneath. These colour contrasts work well. Dark colour stands out but is not overpowering.
This wasn't on brand. Just a random system that got built one day, and I kept it because I loved it. Not my brand, but it uses colour really well without any colour ever overpowering.
I like the yellow bar at the bottom. Fine with the Mary vs John colours even if a bit weird. Like that you can see progress from the brief PRD.

Good use of colour throughout. The activation command feels a bit overdone, with so many different colours used for Analysis, Create Product Brief, Planning. Useful to see the sections, but the colours feel a bit too flamboyant.
Really like this. Don't love my brand light brown, but it's clear. Really clean. Not much use of colour for data/semantic, but where used, very effective.
The colour for POST and GET is great. Common requests render template, everything subtle. You can see the stuff that matters (the JSON object).
Dynamic in the real world; hover turns yellow, felt like an interactive widget. Cool.
Relatively good use of background colours for the yellow and green. Great heading at the top.
Not bad. Liked the BMAD question in the dotted line. Four different concepts so it makes sense each has its own colour.
I love the 91% pass rate graphic.
The whole page looks a bit harsh; it's the big green banner doing it.
It looks a bit childish because of the way colours are being used.
Nice use of different colours (yellows, greys, greens). They match the percentages: high, n/a, midline. Good discernment by the agent.
Strong use of two different ideas that align. Manual workflow vs CI/CD automation; manual workflow in particular is wonderful.
Good use of showing commands people can run, keeping it separate by section.
Conceptually okay, but a bit too childish, too many colours, too many big bold colours.
This is better. Background much more subtle. Only green made it in, everything else grey, with a bit of semantic colour to show what stage you're up to. Very good.
Not that great a screen. Ok, not great.
Good use of two colour left and right; like the key differences below.
An OK way of showing the different packages.
Maybe a little too overboard with the background colours.
Works using the colours like this. They only use each colour once, which is fine. Like that the tiny bullet points match the heading.
Too many colours going on in this.
Nice and subtle. This is good.
Pretty good for showing what looks like some sort of schema.
OK. Sometimes the colours are too bold. Not wrong, but could be toned down with transparency. Don't make that a rule, just pointing it out.
Nice, works well. Feels like a brand-new style again, a bit challenging for consistency.
Using lighter tones and downplaying the colours; the real issue is mixing all the colours with the brand colours.
Really useful view of an environment file. A bit harsh with the user and development colours.
Colours like this are fine, just a little too heavy.
This just looks broken and horrible.
Clean and stylish. Like that one of them is again a random colour, just to go 'hey, look at me, I'm interesting.'
Just looks horrible.
Pretty good. Like Bob/James/Quinn with icons and details. Like the orange at the bottom; interesting use but feels a bit messy too.
Okay, but now the orange is getting a little too heavy.
It's okay.
Too much green. It just feels a bit wrong.
Pretty nice overall. A few contrast issues, especially dark brown against blue. Showing brand brown on blue is probably half the challenge.
This is okay.
Purple all the way through like this isn't the best.
Little bit too much yellow.
Just bad contrasting colours.
Too much green and bad contrasting colours.
Too much green, bad contrasting colours.
Too much green, bad contrast in colours.
Nice progress bar and hyperlinks. Good use of yellow on dark brown, white and light brown on dark brown. Apart from that the screen is very busy.
Handy way of looking at functional requirements.
Not a bad way of showing things in three groups.
Problem vs solution is nice. The purple is a bit full on.
The three softer colours are pretty good for the background.
OK with the light blues.
Purple doesn't look so good when it's all on its own.
Don't know if this is meant to be all about arrows; pretty bright and full on.
Too much green.
Ugly pairing of colours.
Pretty good colour contrast, even though not happy about my old brand colours.
Having one thing stick out, the killer feature, is a good touch.
Too much colour going on.
Nice separation of concepts. Proposed config looking like a config is great.
OK, but the maintenance backlog headings (H1/H2) look off.
OK side-by-side list. Like that out of scope is grey. Like the brand-coloured MVP success criterion summary at the bottom.
A little busy, but ok.
Looks messy with the way the colours are done.
The red is a bit overboard.
Too many bold colours.
OK. The green on that brand is ugly, but the rest is ok.
Pretty nice. Using brand colours only, almost, which is cool.
An old version. Nice but doesn't matter.
ugly
old version, don't take too much from this
Too many colours.
Nice use of side-by-side actions or agents.
Pretty good.
Pretty good. Like the three colours for core app and POEM.
Semi-useful chart of implementation.
Good side-by-side checklist.
Good use of examples in different languages. Really good.
Good use of colour.
Good use of colour.
Like that little title case / upper case / lower case stuff in the top right hand corner.
Like that 100 quality score certified.
Not a bad scan, 100% complete. It's okay.
Very good use of showing how to run a command and click copy on it. A standardised system you see out there.
Nice little charts for success / unit test sort of stuff.
Nice scorecard, with the security badges below, performance reliability.
Clean, simple colours here.
Good example of completion and pass.
